verses.book: Dialogue III. The Virtues of Martin Continued.
verses.text: “When this became known to Martin, he rushed to the palace, though it was now night.

verses.book: Dialogue III. The Virtues of Martin Continued.
verses.text: He pledges himself that, if these people were spared, he would communicate; only let the tribunes, who had already been sent to the Spains for the destruction of the churches, be recalled.

verses.book: Dialogue III. The Virtues of Martin Continued.
verses.text: There is no delay: Maximus grants all his requests.

verses.book: Dialogue III. The Virtues of Martin Continued.
verses.text: On the following day, the ordination of Felix as bishop was being arranged, a man undoubtedly of great sanctity, and truly worthy of being made a priest in happier times.

verses.book: Dialogue III. The Virtues of Martin Continued.
verses.text: Martin took part in the communion of that day, judging it better to yield for the moment, than to disregard the safety of those over whose heads a sword was hanging.

verses.book: Dialogue III. The Virtues of Martin Continued.
verses.text: Nevertheless, although the bishops strove to the uttermost to get him to confirm the fact of his communicating by signing his name, he could not be induced to do so.

verses.book: Dialogue III. The Virtues of Martin Continued.
verses.text: On the following day, hurrying away from that place, as he was on the way returning, he was filled with mourning and lamentation that he had even for an hour been mixed up with the evil communion, and, not far from a village named Andethanna, where remote woods stretch far and wide with profound solitude, he sat down while his companions went on a little before him.

verses.book: Dialogue III. The Virtues of Martin Continued.
verses.text: There he became involved in deep thought, alternately accusing and defending the cause of his grief and conduct.

verses.book: Dialogue III. The Virtues of Martin Continued.
verses.text: Suddenly, an angel stood by him and said, ‘Justly, O Martin, do you feel compunction, but you could not otherwise get out of your difficulty.

verses.book: Dialogue III. The Virtues of Martin Continued.
verses.text: Renew your virtue, resume your courage, lest you not only now expose your fame, but your very salvation, to danger.’

verses.book: Dialogue III. The Virtues of Martin Continued.
verses.text: Therefore, from that time forward, he carefully guarded against being mixed up in communion with the party of Ithacius.

verses.book: Dialogue III. The Virtues of Martin Continued.
verses.text: But when it happened that he cured some of the possessed more slowly and with less grace than usual, he at once confessed to us with tears that he felt a diminution of his power on account of the evil of that communion in which he had taken part for a moment through necessity, and not with a cordial spirit.

verses.book: Dialogue III. The Virtues of Martin Continued.
verses.text: He lived sixteen years after this, but never again did he attend a synod, and kept carefully aloof from all assemblies of bishops.
